deterministicCategoryRules.intro: Rules that assign a category from the email sender, subject, and body without calling the categorisation model. Composite rules (all conditions must match) are evaluated before legacy auto-generated rules.
deterministicCategoryRules.empty: No deterministic rules for this account yet.
deterministicCategoryRules.brokenTooltip: This rule has no linked category, so it can never assign one. Edit it to pick a category, or delete it.
deterministicCategoryRules.categoryHelp: Pick the category this rule files matching email into. Manage your categories in Guide our AI.
deterministicCategoryRules.senderField: Sender(s) — one per line, any match
deterministicCategoryRules.senderHelp: Enter one sender email address per line. If the email matches ANY of these senders, the sender condition is satisfied.
deterministicCategoryRules.subjectContainsField: Subject contains — one phrase per line, any match
deterministicCategoryRules.subjectHelp: Enter one subject phrase per line (case-insensitive). If the subject contains ANY of these phrases, the subject condition is satisfied.
deterministicCategoryRules.bodyPhrasesField: Body contains — one phrase per line, any match
deterministicCategoryRules.bodyPhrasesHelp: Plain text from the email body is searched (case-insensitive). Any non-empty line that appears in the message satisfies the body condition.
deterministicCategoryRules.subjectNotContainsField: Subject does NOT contain — one phrase per line (optional)
deterministicCategoryRules.subjectNotContainsHelp: Optional. The rule fails to match if ANY of these phrases appears in the subject. Use to disambiguate from closely-related categories (e.g. exclude "Issue #" so a PR-specific rule does not match GitHub issue notifications).
deterministicCategoryRules.bodyNotContainsField: Body does NOT contain — one phrase per line (optional)
deterministicCategoryRules.bodyNotContainsHelp: Optional. The rule fails to match if ANY of these phrases appears in the body. Useful when the same sender produces both wanted and unwanted notifications.
deterministicCategoryRules.legacyWeakWarning: This rule matches on limited criteria and may produce false positives. Consider creating a composite rule.
deterministicCategoryRules.allFieldsRequired: All three fields (sender, subject, body) are required to reduce false positives.
deterministicCategoryRules.fieldRequiredError: This field is required. Enter at least one value.
deterministicCategoryRules.notContainsRequiredError: Add at least one NOT-contains phrase (subject or body) so the rule does not match too broadly.
deterministicCategoryRules.addRuleChoiceDescription: How would you like to create a new rule?
deterministicCategoryRules.addRuleSuggestDescription: Analyse your email history and draft rules for senders with at least 5 threads. You confirm before anything is saved.
deterministicCategoryRules.addRuleManualDescription: Open a blank form to define sender, subject, and body conditions yourself.
deterministicCategoryRules.suggestEmpty: No suggestions found. You may not have enough email history yet (at least 5 threads per sender required).
deterministicCategoryRules.suggestError: Could not load suggestions. Please try again.
deterministicCategoryRules.suggestIntro: These rules are drafted from your email history. Click "Review" to open the form and edit before saving.

priorityRules.intro: When a sender's emails have consistently received the same priority, we learn a rule so future emails from them are prioritised instantly — without calling the AI. Disable any rule you disagree with.

reencryption.title: KMS data re-encryption
reencryption.description: Re-encrypts user data from the legacy global ENCRYPTION_KEY to the per-user KMS-derived data key. Idempotent — already-migrated rows are skipped. Use Dry-run self first to verify decryption works on your own data; then Start dry-run all to preview impact across all users; then Start real run when ready.
reencryption.dryRunComplete: Dry-run complete. See per-table breakdown below.
reencryption.dryRunFailed: Dry-run job failed. Check worker logs for the failing table and row.

reencryption.fanoutFailed: Fan-out job failed. Check worker logs.
reencryption.fanoutNoResult: Fan-out job ended in state "{{state}}" without a result. Check worker logs.
reencryption.startAllConfirm: This will re-encrypt every pending user's data. Continue?

health.rowsNeedingRemediationHint: Rows with a value stored as plaintext (bypassed encryption). This is the real signal — a clean account scores 0.
health.jobVisitedUsersHint: Only tracks whether the re-encryption job ran for a user — NOT whether their data is encrypted at rest.
health.breakdownSubtitle: Per encrypted column: how many stored values are plaintext (not encrypted-shaped) and need remediation.
health.allEncrypted: Every scanned value is encrypted at rest. Nothing to remediate. ✅
health.scanFailed: Health scan job ended in state "{{state}}" without a result. Check worker logs.
health.scannedNote: Scanned {{tables}} encrypted tables. Detects plaintext at rest; the per-key (global→user) check still requires a dry-run.

fanout.polling: Waiting for per-user jobs to complete ({{terminal}}/{{total}})…
fanout.noFailures: No row-level failures across any user. ✅
fanout.childJobErrorsDescription: These per-user jobs did not complete successfully (failed, expired, cancelled, or pruned). Use the raw output preview to diagnose unfamiliar error shapes, and cross-reference with worker logs.
fanout.childJobErrorsNoDetailWarning: {{count}} job(s) failed but the server returned no per-job error details. This usually means the backend image hasn't picked up the latest aggregation fix yet — try Refresh in a moment, or check worker logs.
fanout.childJobErrorFallbackMessage: Child did not complete (state: {{state}}). Server did not provide a per-job message; check worker logs for context.

contactTokens.title: Contact search-token backfill
contactTokens.description: Regenerates the blind-index searchTokens for every user's contacts that have NULL/empty tokens, so contacts synced before the search feature become findable. Runs as one background job under each user's KMS key. Idempotent — only NULL/empty rows are touched. Use Dry-run to preview impact, then Start real run.
contactTokens.startConfirm: This will regenerate search tokens for every user with NULL/empty contact tokens. Continue?
contactTokens.failed: Backfill job failed. Check worker logs.
contactTokens.noResult: Backfill job ended in state "{{state}}" without a result. Check worker logs.

categoryRuleIds.title: Category-rule ID backfill
categoryRuleIds.description: Links each category rule to its category by stable ID (categoryId FK) instead of the encrypted name, so renaming a category no longer breaks its rules. Backfills rules created before the FK existed. Runs as one background job under each user's KMS key. Idempotent — only rules with no categoryId are touched. Use Dry-run to preview, then Start real run.
categoryRuleIds.startConfirm: This will link every unlinked category rule to its category. Continue?
categoryRuleIds.failed: Backfill job failed. Check worker logs.
categoryRuleIds.noResult: Backfill job ended in state "{{state}}" without a result. Check worker logs.

help.title: Help
help.description: Find answers to common questions and learn how to use BearlyMail effectively.

context.title: Context About Me
context.description: Learn how to provide context to help BearlyMail understand your priorities and preferences.

section0.title: Why Context Matters
section0.content: BearlyMail uses AI to prioritize your emails and help you focus on what matters most. By providing context about yourself, your work, and your preferences, you help the AI make better decisions about which emails need your immediate attention and which can wait.

section1.title: VIP Contacts
section1.content: Add people whose emails should always be treated as high priority. This could include your manager, key clients, family members, or anyone whose messages you never want to miss. Emails from VIP contacts will be prioritized higher in your inbox.

section2.title: Info About Me
section2.content: Share information about your role, responsibilities, and work context. For example, if you're a product manager working on a mobile app launch, the AI will better understand which emails relate to your core responsibilities.

section3.title: Current Projects & Topics
section3.content: List the projects, topics, or initiatives you're currently focused on. Emails related to these topics will be prioritized accordingly. Update this list as your priorities change.

section4.title: What I Consider Urgent
section4.content: Define what urgency means to you. This could include specific keywords, types of requests, or situations that require immediate attention. The AI uses this to identify truly urgent emails that should break through batching.

section5.title: What I Don't Consider Important
section5.content: Specify types of emails that are low priority for you. This might include newsletters, marketing emails, or certain types of notifications. These emails will be deprioritized in your inbox.

section6.title: Tips for Better Context
section6.item0: Be specific - instead of 'work emails', say 'emails about the Q1 product launch'
section6.item1: Update your context regularly as your priorities change
section6.item2: Use the 'Analyze Emails' feature to let BearlyMail learn from your email history
section6.item3: Review and adjust VIP contacts based on who you actually respond to quickly

triage.title: Triage Tab
triage.description: Understand how to quickly sort through new emails in the Triage tab.

section0.title: What is the Triage Tab?
section0.content: The Triage tab is where new emails arrive for quick decision-making. Instead of letting emails pile up in a traditional inbox, BearlyMail delivers them in batches so you can efficiently decide what to do with each one without constant interruptions.

section1.title: How Email Batching Works
section1.content: New emails are collected and delivered at scheduled times throughout the day based on your settings. This prevents the constant distraction of incoming emails and lets you focus on deep work. Truly urgent emails can still break through batching when needed.

section2.title: Priority Scoring
section2.content: Each email in Triage is assigned a priority score from 0-100 based on the sender, subject, content, and your context settings. Higher scores indicate more important emails. You can click on the priority score to see why it was assigned and provide feedback to improve future scoring.

section3.title: Quick Actions
section3.content: For each email in Triage, you can quickly star it (to move it to Process for later action), archive it, snooze it for later, or open it to read and reply. Use keyboard shortcuts for even faster processing.

section4.title: Starring Emails
section4.content: When you star an email, it moves to the Process tab where you can give it your full attention. Use stars for emails that need a thoughtful response or action. You can use 1-5 stars to indicate different priority levels.

section5.title: Tips for Efficient Triage
section5.item0: Process emails in batches rather than one at a time throughout the day
section5.item1: Use keyboard shortcuts: Delete to archive, 1-5 for stars
section5.item2: Don't overthink - make quick decisions and move on
section5.item3: Star anything that needs more than 2 minutes of attention

process.title: Process Tab
process.description: Learn how to work through starred emails that need action in the Process tab.

section0.title: What is the Process Tab?
section0.content: The Process tab is your focused workspace for emails that need thoughtful attention. When you star an email in Triage, it moves here so you can deal with it properly without the distraction of other incoming emails.

section1.title: Working Through Starred Emails
section1.content: Emails in the Process tab are sorted by their star rating and priority score. Work through them one at a time, giving each email the attention it deserves. Once you've replied or taken action, archive the email to remove it from your list.

section2.title: AI-Powered Summaries
section2.content: For each email, BearlyMail can generate AI summaries to help you quickly understand the content. Choose from TL;DR, bullet points, action items, or sender request summaries. You can also create custom summarization rules for specific types of emails.

section3.title: Drafting Replies
section3.content: Use the reply composer to draft your response. BearlyMail includes a tone checker that reviews your draft against your writing preferences and suggests improvements. You can also get AI-suggested replies to help you get started.

section4.title: Action Items
section4.content: The AI can extract action items from emails, helping you identify what needs to be done. You can add your own tasks, mark items as complete, and keep track of what you've committed to.

section5.title: Tips for Effective Processing
section5.item0: Set aside dedicated time blocks for processing emails
section5.item1: Use AI summaries to quickly understand long email threads
section5.item2: Archive emails immediately after taking action
section5.item3: Use the tone checker before sending important replies

followUp.title: Follow Up Tab
followUp.description: Discover how to manage and send follow-up emails to conversations waiting for responses.

section0.title: What is the Follow Up Tab?
section0.content: The Follow Up tab helps you track conversations where you're waiting for a response. When you send an email and expect a reply, BearlyMail monitors the thread and reminds you to follow up if you haven't heard back.

section1.title: Setting Follow-Up Expectations
section1.content: When sending a reply, you can specify when you expect to hear back. Options include hours, days, or a custom timeframe. If no response arrives by that time, the conversation appears in your Follow Up tab.

section2.title: Managing Pending Follow-Ups
section2.content: The Follow Up tab shows all conversations awaiting responses, sorted by how long you've been waiting. You can see who you're waiting on, when you last sent a message, and how many days have passed since their last response.

section3.title: Generating Follow-Up Messages
section3.content: BearlyMail can generate follow-up messages for you using AI. Click 'Generate Follow-ups' to create draft messages for all pending conversations, or generate them individually. Review and customize each draft before sending.

section4.title: Bulk Follow-Up Actions
section4.content: For efficiency, you can send multiple follow-up emails at once. Select the conversations you want to follow up on, review the generated drafts, and send them in bulk. This is especially useful for sales or outreach workflows.

section5.title: Tips for Effective Follow-Ups
section5.item0: Set realistic follow-up expectations based on the urgency of your request
section5.item1: Review AI-generated follow-ups before sending to ensure they're appropriate
section5.item2: Don't follow up too aggressively - space out your reminders
section5.item3: Remove conversations from follow-up if they're no longer relevant

search.title: Search
search.description: Master the search functionality to quickly find emails using natural language or Gmail syntax.

section0.title: Searching Your Emails
section0.content: BearlyMail's search feature lets you quickly find any email using natural language questions or traditional Gmail search syntax. The AI understands what you're looking for and returns the most relevant results.

section1.title: Natural Language Search
section1.content: Simply type what you're looking for in plain English. For example, 'emails from John about the budget' or 'meeting invites from last week'. The AI interprets your query and finds matching emails.

section2.title: Gmail Search Syntax
section2.content: You can also use Gmail's powerful search operators for precise queries. Common operators include from:, to:, subject:, has:attachment, before:, after:, and more. Combine multiple operators for complex searches.

section3.title: Search Operators Reference
section3.item0: from:name - Emails from a specific sender
section3.item1: to:name - Emails sent to a specific recipient
section3.item2: subject:text - Emails with specific words in the subject
section3.item4: before:date / after:date - Emails before or after a date

section4.title: Search Results
section4.content: Search results are ranked by relevance, with a score showing how well each email matches your query. Recent emails get a slight boost in ranking. Click on any result to view the full email.

section5.title: Tips for Better Searches
section5.item0: Start with natural language - it's often faster than building complex queries
section5.item1: Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "project proposal"
section5.item3: Use the search help button for a quick reference of available operators

settings.title: Settings
settings.description: Configure email delivery, AI preferences, and integrations to customize your BearlyMail experience.

section0.title: Settings Overview
section0.content: The Settings page lets you customize every aspect of your BearlyMail experience. From email delivery schedules to AI behavior, you have full control over how BearlyMail works for you.

section1.title: Email Delivery & Batching
section1.content: Control when and how often emails are delivered to your inbox. Set batch delivery times, configure quiet hours when no emails should be delivered, and choose how many times per day you want to receive email batches.

section2.title: Google Accounts
section2.content: Connect and manage your Gmail accounts. You can link multiple accounts and BearlyMail will sync emails from all of them. If sync issues occur, you can reconnect your account from here.

section3.title: Blocked Senders
section3.content: Manage your list of blocked senders. Emails from blocked addresses are automatically archived and won't appear in your Triage. You can also unsubscribe from mailing lists directly.

section4.title: Guide Our AI
section4.content: This is where you provide context about yourself to help the AI prioritize your emails. Add VIP contacts, describe your current projects, define what's urgent to you, and specify what's not important.

section5.title: Tone Settings
section5.content: Configure rules for the tone checker that reviews your email drafts. Add rules like 'Be professional' or 'Avoid passive-aggressive language'. The AI learns from your sent emails to understand your writing style.

section6.title: Summarization Rules
section6.content: Create custom rules for how emails should be summarized. For example, you might want meeting requests summarized as bullet points, or emails from your manager to highlight action items only.

section7.title: Integrations
section7.content: Connect BearlyMail with other services. Add your own OpenAI API key for AI features, connect GitHub to see PR and issue status directly in emails, and more integrations coming soon.

section8.title: Tips for Optimal Settings
section8.item0: Start with 2-3 email batches per day and adjust based on your needs
section8.item1: Set quiet hours to match your focus time or after-work hours
section8.item2: Regularly update your context as your priorities change
section8.item3: Use the tone checker to maintain consistent professional communication

autoresponder.title: Auto-Responder
autoresponder.description: Learn how to set up automatic responses to incoming emails with queue status and AI-powered answers.

section0.title: What is the Auto-Responder?
section0.content: The Auto-Responder automatically sends replies to incoming emails, letting senders know their message has been received and providing helpful information about when they can expect a response. It can also answer common questions using AI based on the Q&A context you've configured.

section1.title: Enabling the Auto-Responder
section1.content: To enable the Auto-Responder, go to Settings and scroll to the Auto-Responder section. Toggle the switch to enable automatic responses. Once enabled, you'll see additional configuration options.

section2.title: Customizing Response Templates
section2.content: You can customize the message templates that are sent for different priority levels. Use merge tags like {{userName}} and {{queuePosition}} to personalize responses with dynamic information. The template editor shows a live preview of how your message will appear.

section3.title: Exclusion Rules
section3.content: Not every email should receive an auto-response. Use exclusion rules to prevent auto-responses from being sent to certain senders or types of emails. For example, you might want to exclude newsletters, automated notifications, or emails from specific domains.

section4.title: AI-Powered Answers
section4.content: When enabled, the Auto-Responder can attempt to answer common questions based on the Q&A pairs in your context settings. You can set a minimum confidence threshold to control how certain the AI must be before including an answer. All AI-generated answers include a disclaimer that you will confirm the information.

section5.title: Best Practices
section5.item0: Keep your response templates professional and concise
section5.item1: Set up exclusion rules for newsletters and automated emails
section5.item2: Regularly review and update your Q&A context for accurate AI answers
section5.item3: Use the email preview feature to test how responses will look before enabling

emailIsBroken.heading: Email Is Broken
emailIsBroken.subheading: Email clients are built to distract you
emailIsBroken.paragraph: Most email tools are designed to keep you checking. They interrupt your thinking. They treat your attention as something to capture, not protect.

newestNotImportant.heading: The newest email is not the most important
newestNotImportant.paragraph1: Email clients put the latest message at the top by default. Important emails don't stop being important just because they arrived yesterday. Critical messages get buried while noise floats up.
newestNotImportant.paragraph2: So you're forced to decide, again and again, what actually matters. Every glance at your inbox becomes a prioritisation exercise.

notTimeSensitive.heading: Not every email is time-sensitive
notTimeSensitive.paragraph1: A Black Friday sale can wait. A newsletter can wait.
notTimeSensitive.paragraph2: But your inbox shows them the moment the sender hits send. Everything arrives immediately.

webelieve.heading: We believe this must change
webelieve.paragraph1: Email should work on your terms. Your inbox should understand what matters to you. Your attention should be protected by default.
webelieve.paragraph2: That belief is why BearlyMail exists.

mobile.paragraph1: I have ADHD, and email was quietly destroying my ability to focus.
mobile.paragraph2: Every new email gave me a dopamine hit. It could be a new customer, an investor, something exciting - and that possibility kept pulling me back to my inbox.
mobile.paragraph3: Most of the time, it wasn't exciting at all. It was emails that needed effort. Thoughtful replies. Decisions. Follow-ups. Those were harder to start, so they stacked up.
mobile.paragraph4: So I built BearlyMail. Now, non-urgent emails arrive in scheduled batches, at times I've already decided to deal with email. Truly urgent messages still break through immediately. My inbox is no longer a slot machine. It's a planned task.

desktop.paragraph1: I have ADHD, and email was quietly destroying my ability to focus.
desktop.paragraph2: Every new email gave me a dopamine hit. It could be a new customer, an investor, something exciting - and that possibility kept pulling me back to my inbox.
desktop.paragraph3: Most of the time, it wasn't exciting at all.
desktop.paragraph4: It was emails that needed effort. Thoughtful replies. Decisions. Follow-ups. Those were harder to start, so they stacked up. Meanwhile, my inbox showed me the newest messages first, even though the important ones were buried somewhere in the middle.
desktop.paragraph5: My brain couldn't tell what mattered at a glance. Everything felt urgent. Or overwhelming. Or both.
desktop.paragraph6: So I checked again. And again.
desktop.paragraph7: I tried all the usual advice. Inbox zero. Filters. Fancy email clients. They helped a bit, but they missed the real problem. Email arrives when other people want my attention, not when I'm ready to deal with it.
desktop.paragraph9: Now, non-urgent emails arrive in scheduled batches, at times I've already decided to deal with email. Truly urgent messages still break through immediately. My inbox is no longer a slot machine. It's a planned task.

faq.title: A few things you might be wondering.

timeSensitive.q: What if I miss something genuinely time-sensitive?
timeSensitive.a: Truly urgent emails break through immediately. The model learns what "urgent" means for you by watching how quickly you reply, who you prioritise, and what you archive without reading. The bar for breaking through is high by design, so only things that genuinely interrupt your flow get through.

priorityScore.q: How is the priority score calculated?
priorityScore.a: It's learned from your context, not a generic formula. The score weighs who's sending — including the VIP senders you mark yourself — the projects and goals you're currently working on, deadlines, and urgency signals in the message. If a score looks wrong, you can correct it, and BearlyMail learns from every correction.

lowScore.q: What if something important gets a low score?
lowScore.a: There are safety nets. You can mark important senders as VIPs so they're never buried, urgent-detection lets time-critical email bypass batching entirely, and nothing is ever hidden — you can always see every email and sort by time in the app.

vip.q: Can I mark important senders myself?
vip.a: Yes. VIP senders are fully user-editable — add or remove anyone at any time. BearlyMail also learns VIPs automatically from how you star and reply, but you always have the final say.

manualTriage.q: Why do I still triage manually if emails already have scores?
manualTriage.a: Scores tell you what's likely to matter; triage tells BearlyMail what actually needs action from you. Starring an email moves it to your Action list, ordered by priority so the most important work sits on top — and the system learns from every choice you make.

colleagues.q: Will my colleagues notice I’m slower to reply?
colleagues.a: Probably not. Most replies that feel "instant" are really under an hour, which still happens with 3× daily delivery. For people you always want to hear from immediately, mark them as VIPs and their email skips the batch.

providers.q: Will it work with Gmail, Outlook, or iCloud?
providers.a: Yes. BearlyMail sits on top of your existing account. No migration, no new email address. Gmail and Microsoft 365 are supported at launch; iCloud and Fastmail follow shortly after.

calendar.q: Does it work with my calendar?
calendar.a: Yes. BearlyMail integrates with Google Calendar for meeting scheduling, so when an email turns into a meeting it can check your availability and help you schedule it without leaving your inbox.

peek.q: Can I peek at what’s being held?
peek.a: Technically yes, by opening Gmail or Outlook directly. We recommend blocking those sites and apps during focus time using <focusBear>Focus Bear</focusBear> so the temptation doesn't pull you back in.

ai.q: Is my email content sent to an AI?
ai.a: Yes, that's how summaries and prioritisation work. We use OpenAI and Gemini under contracts that block training on your data. Nothing is used to improve their models, and we never sell data. Full export and full delete, any time.

gmailImportant.q: How is this different from Gmail’s "Important" or Snooze?
gmailImportant.a: Gmail's Important label still shows you everything immediately, just sorted differently. Snooze still relies on you to triage every email first. BearlyMail flips the default: nothing arrives unless you've decided to be interrupted, or unless it's actually urgent.

price.a: From $15 USD / month at launch. Waitlist members get a lifetime discount and the first month free.

cancel.a: Nothing is lost. BearlyMail sits on top of Gmail or Outlook, so every email is still there in your normal account. Cancel any time and your inbox goes back to standard delivery.
